:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commitments vs. Parole Revocations

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. Understanding the relative proportion of these two groups is pivotal for evaluating the effectiveness of a state's criminal justice policies.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Most felony offenders receive indeterminate sentences that contain a minimum and a maximum term. The maximum term may not be greater than the maximum provided by law, and the minimum term may not be less than the minimum provided by law and may not be more than 90% of the maximum term.10 Offenders with indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole when the minimum term imposed by the court, minus good time earned, is complete. When an inmate begins imprisonment, the Department of Corrections calculates a projected parole eligibility date based on the full potential good time allowance and the minimum sentence. This calculation is not “an award of or entitlement to good time but is merely for administrative purposes.” Inmates who have committed an escape, attempted escape, or assisted others to escape from any institution during a sentence are ineligible for parole on that sentence. Inmates who have committed assault with a deadly weapon on another inmate, an officer, or a staff member during a sentence are also ineligible for parole during that sentence.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 522 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes, with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 25% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 77% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 88% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 31% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 5-9.9 years, accounting for 32% of this group.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to rape or sexual assault, accounting for 32% of this group.


``` ::: :::

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::

:::: ::: citation-section **Notes:** NCRP data is from 2020. The parole-eligible prison population only includes people with sentence lengths ranging between 1-25 years and people incarcerated for a new crime.
